title: Post-Summit Payoff Push: Turkey Presses F-35 and Sanctions Relief as Defense Realignment Takes Shape
subhead: A week after the Ankara NATO summit, Turkey is moving fast to convert Trump's promises into concrete gains — FM Fidan says sanctions and F-35 restrictions should lift 'soon,' while France signals openness to SAMP/T air defense sales. But Congress, Cyprus, and the unresolved S-400 question still stand in the way.

sections.heading: Fidan Presses for Sanctions Relief and F-35 Return After Trump's Promise
sections.body:
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said on July 10 that Turkey 'hopes to achieve a result on lifting U.S. sanctions and its desire to purchase F-35 fighter jets soon,' adding that 'there should be no defence industry restrictions between allies,' Reuters reports. The statement came three days after President Trump announced at the NATO summit that he would lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and consider selling F-35s to Ankara, calling Turkey 'much more loyal than other countries' and praising his relationship with Erdogan.

Trump's pledge at the Ankara summit was striking: he said he 'might not have attended' the summit if it hadn't been in Turkey, and compared his bond with Erdogan favorably against other leaders: 'Sometimes you get along with the toughest people, like Erdogan. And sometimes you don't get along with the weakest, most pathetic people, maybe you don't respect them,' The Guardian reports. NATO Ambassador Whitaker separately discussed the potential F-35 sale, noting that Turkey would likely need to address the S-400 issue to proceed.

But turning presidential rhetoric into policy faces significant hurdles. CAATSA sanctions were imposed by Congress, not the White House, and any F-35 sale would require congressional approval. The S-400 Russian air defense system — the original reason Turkey was expelled from the F-35 program in 2019 — remains operational. Daily Sabah noted that the late Senator Lindsey Graham, who died on July 12 at age 71, had evolved from a hawkish critic of Turkey to a Trump-aligned supporter, and his absence removes one Republican voice that had tracked Turkey policy closely. Reuters confirmed Graham's death from a heart ailment caused by hardening of the arteries.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's personal guarantee doesn't override congressional authority, and the S-400 hasn't gone anywhere. Turkey is trying to lock in commitments while Trump's political capital is hot, but the institutional barriers — Congress, the S-400's continued presence, and Greek/Cypriot opposition — are formidable.

sections.heading: France Opens Door to SAMP/T Sale as Turkey's Defense Pivot Accelerates
sections.body:
On the European side of the defense realignment, France has signaled openness to selling the Franco-Italian SAMP/T air defense system to Turkey, according to five sources familiar with the matter cited by Reuters on July 6. The shift ends years of French political opposition to such a sale, driven in part by tensions between Paris and Ankara over the Eastern Mediterranean and Libya. The SAMP/T, manufactured by the Eurosam consortium (MBDA Italy and MBDA France), would give Turkey a NATO-interoperable long-range air defense capability — something the S-400 cannot provide.

Al-Monitor reports that Turkey is seeking to turn the diplomatic momentum from the Ankara NATO summit into a broad defense realignment spanning U.S. and European procurement. The SAMP/T discussions and the F-35 push represent two tracks of the same strategy: replacing Russian systems with Western ones, and converting tactical leverage into institutional access. But the Al-Monitor piece was behind a paywall, and details on the SAMP/T conditions — including whether Greece or Cyprus could exercise a veto — were not publicly available.

Separately, Turkey is still evaluating participation in Canada's Defence Security and Resilience Bank (DSRB), defense ministry sources told Reuters on July 11. The DSRB is a new multilateral defense financing mechanism, and Turkey's hesitation suggests it is weighing the terms against its own defense-industrial interests and existing bilateral arrangements.
sections.whyItMatters: The SAMP/T opening is the most tangible sign that France's blockade on Turkish defense access is softening — but the terms, conditions, and political price Turkey would pay remain unclear. If Turkey gets both F-35s and SAMP/T, it would effectively complete its pivot away from Russian air defense and back into the Western fold. If either deal stalls, the S-400 stays as an unresolved strategic liability.

sections.heading: Trump's Volatile NATO Performance: Turkey Wins the Stage, Allies Lose Sleep
sections.body:
Analysis of the NATO summit continues to reverberate. The Guardian's post-summit assessment described Trump's performance as having a 'bipolar quality,' driven in part by his personal chemistry with Erdogan. Ian Lesser of the German Marshall Fund, who attended the summit, said Trump 'was keen to be sure that President Erdogan was able to claim a success from the summit,' and that the personal relationship 'probably played a role in stabilizing things.' But Lesser also noted Trump 'has a tendency to see the world through not so much through alliances, but through individual countries and, above all, individual leaders.'

Trump arrived at the summit visibly angry about the Iran ceasefire falling apart, threatening further destruction and calling Iran's leadership 'scum.' Hours later, he declared 'a lot of love in that room' and praised Zelenskyy as 'ingenious.' The Guardian quoted Georgetown professor Charles Kupchan noting that despite Trump's rhetoric, 'NATO is still NATO. There are still 80,000 US troops in Europe.' But Kupchan acknowledged that European allies will remain 'freaked out' by Trump's hostile language and unpredictability.

A Firstpost analysis framed the summit as marking 'NATO 3.0' — a paradigm shift where American leadership becomes 'selective rather than automatic' and European responsibility becomes 'structural rather than optional.' The analysis noted that Turkey's role as host was more than symbolic: 'Turkey isn't just hosting NATO 3.0; it is previewing what NATO 3.0 looks like in practice: a member state contributing more capability while exercising more independent foreign policy.' The Bulwark's Eric Edelman highlighted the 'conspiracy of silence' at the summit regarding Turkey's democratic backsliding, noting that no leader raised concerns about Erdogan's crackdown on opposition or press freedom.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey extracted maximum diplomatic value from hosting the summit — Erdogan got a personal endorsement from Trump and a platform to argue for defense integration. But Trump's volatility means the promises made in Ankara could evaporate with the next tweet, and the summit's deeper legacy may be accelerating European strategic autonomy rather than solidifying Turkey's position within it.

sections.heading: Turkey-Iraq Oil Pipeline Extended for 12 Months as Energy Routes Stabilize
sections.body:
Turkey and Iraq are set to sign a one-year extension of the agreement keeping the Iraq-Turkey crude oil pipeline operational, Turkish Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar announced on July 9, according to Reuters. The pipeline, which runs from northern Iraq to Turkey's Mediterranean port of Ceyhan, had been disrupted for months following a 2023 International Court of Arbitration ruling and subsequent disputes over Kurdish regional exports. The 12-month extension provides temporary certainty, but the underlying legal and political questions — including the status of Kurdish oil exports and Baghdad-Erbil revenue-sharing — remain unresolved.

The extension comes amid broader energy-route upheaval. The Strait of Hormuz remains contested, with Iran declaring it closed and the U.S. disputing that claim, as Bloomberg reported that the southern route remained open to shipping on July 12 despite Iran's declaration. Oil prices have been volatile, rising 4% on week-of-July-7 Hormuz disruption fears and settling 2% lower on July 10 as economic worries outweighed supply concerns, per Reuters. Saudi Arabia is considering expanding its Red Sea pipeline capacity to bypass Hormuz entirely, according to Reuters.

Turkey's energy position is complex: as a net energy importer, it benefits from lower oil prices, but as a transit state, it gains strategic value from pipeline alternatives that bypass Hormuz. The Iraq-Turkey pipeline and the prospective Eastern Mediterranean gas routes give Ankara leverage as an energy corridor state.
sections.whyItMatters: The 12-month pipeline extension is a pragmatic stopgap, not a strategic resolution. But with Hormuz in chaos, every reliable alternative route gains value — and Turkey's Ceyhan port becomes more important to global energy markets with each day the Strait stays contested.

sections.heading: Cyprus Objects to Turkey-Northern Cyprus Gas Pipeline at EU Level
sections.body:
Cyprus Foreign Minister Constantinos Kombos will raise the issue of a natural gas pipeline agreement signed between Turkey and the Turkish Cypriot authorities at an upcoming EU meeting, MSN reports. The agreement foresees the construction of a natural gas pipeline between Turkey and the breakaway Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus (TRNC), which is recognized only by Ankara.

The pipeline project directly competes with Israel's planned 200-kilometer pipeline connecting its Karish and Tanin gas fields (2.4 trillion cubic feet of reserves) to Cyprus, being developed by Energean with a $1.6 billion investment. Israeli Energy Minister Eli Cohen stated that the pipeline 'will be built, rejecting any opposition,' and that Greece and Cyprus have 'no interest' in Turkish participation, according to AGC News. Cohen also warned that Israel would respond to any Turkish military presence in Syria by establishing Israeli bases in the same area.

The Cyprus dispute over Turkey's pipeline adds another dimension to the Eastern Mediterranean energy rivalry. Turkey has long sought a role as a regional energy hub, and the TRNC pipeline is part of that ambition. But it also provides additional ammunition for France and Cyprus to argue against deeper EU-Turkey defense integration — the same political friction that blocks Turkish access to the SAFE program.
sections.whyItMatters: The Cyprus gas pipeline dispute isn't just about energy — it's a direct link between Turkey's regional energy ambitions and its inability to break through EU defense barriers. Every Turkish pipeline deal with the TRNC makes it harder for France and Cyprus to drop their opposition to Turkish participation in European defense programs.

sections.heading: Turkey Suspends Over 100 Doctors Over C-Section Rates
sections.body:
Turkey's Health Ministry has fined and temporarily suspended over 100 obstetrician-gynecologists for performing cesarean sections, also requiring them to undergo additional training, NDTV and SCMP report. The Antalya Chamber of Physicians said doctors were 'issued with warnings, subjected to disciplinary investigations, temporarily suspended from practising, and forced to attend antenatal training courses, on the grounds of high caesarean section rates across the country.'

The action follows the government's April 2025 ban on elective C-sections at private healthcare facilities without medical justification. Turkey has the highest C-section rate among 38 OECD nations — approximately 61.5% of live births in 2023 were by cesarean, according to SCMP. The policy is part of Erdogan's 'Decade of the Family' initiative aimed at boosting birth rates.

Dr. Ayse Gultekingil of the Turkish Medical Association (TTB) said the issue is 'structural,' not individual: 'Turkey's cesarean birth rate exceeds 60 per cent. But the method of delivery reflects various problems within Turkey's healthcare system.' The TTB argues that systemic factors — including inadequate prenatal care, insufficient pain management for natural births, and private hospital financial incentives — drive the high C-section rate, not individual physician decisions.
sections.whyItMatters: The suspension of over 100 doctors over C-section rates is the most aggressive enforcement yet of a policy that pits the government's demographic agenda against medical professionals and women's healthcare choices. It signals Erdogan's willingness to override clinical autonomy in service of pronatalist policy — and it's likely to worsen Turkey's already-strained relationship with its medical community.

sections.heading: Central Bank Holds Steady: July Inflation Data Will Determine Rate Path
sections.body:
Bloomberg reported on July 10 that Turkey's central bank governor Fatih Karahan told investors the bank wants to see more evidence of slowing inflation and clarity on the Iran conflict before resuming monetary easing. Anadolu Agency separately quoted Karahan saying that the 'deterioration in inflation expectations remains limited despite the geopolitical backdrop,' and that residents' demand for Turkish lira 'remains robust' while foreign exchange reserves are 'strong.'

The central bank's cautious stance reflects the dual pressure of Hormuz-driven energy price volatility — which feeds directly into Turkey's import bill as a net energy importer — and a disinflation trajectory that had been progressing before the Iran war disrupted oil markets. The bank has held its policy rate steady for several months after aggressive tightening in 2023-2024 brought inflation down from its peak above 80%.

The July inflation print, expected later this month, will be the key data point determining whether the bank resumes rate cuts. Energy-transmitted inflation from the Hormuz disruption is the primary risk, though Daily Sabah notes that Turkey's economy is in a 'disinflation process' supported by fiscal discipline and defense-led industrial growth.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's disinflation story is real but fragile — and entirely dependent on what happens with oil prices. If Hormuz stabilizes and July inflation continues downward, the bank can resume easing. If energy prices spike again, the entire rate-cut timeline gets pushed back, with implications for growth, lira stability, and Erdogan's political calculus.

bottomLine: The week after the Ankara NATO summit is turning presidential promises into policy tests. Trump said he'd lift sanctions on Turkey and consider F-35 sales; FM Fidan says it should happen 'soon.' France is signaling openness to SAMP/T. But every avenue — F-35s, SAMP/T, EU defense access — runs into the same walls: Congress, the S-400, and Greek/Cypriot opposition. Turkey's defense realignment is real (bilateral deals keep stacking up), but the institutional breakthrough hasn't happened yet. Meanwhile, the Strait of Hormuz crisis makes Turkey's value as an energy corridor more salient by the day, and the central bank's rate-cut timeline hangs on whether July inflation data shows the disinflation trend holding. Erdogan has momentum; converting it into durable policy change is the harder part.
sources: Reuters, The Guardian, Bloomberg, Anadolu Agency, Daily Sabah, NDTV, South China Morning Post, Al-Monitor, Firstpost, The Bulwark, MSN, AGC News, Forbes, Geopolitical Futures, NATO
